items.q: Why are top predators most affected by persistent pollutants like mercury?
items.correct: Biomagnification concentrates pollutants at each trophic level up the food chain.
items.misconception: Predators are exposed to more pollution directly.
items.why: Students focus on direct exposure. The mechanism is concentration through eating contaminated prey - small accumulations in lower levels multiply upward, so apex predators have the highest body burdens.

items.q: How do greenhouse gases warm Earth?
items.correct: They absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate some back down.
items.misconception: They block heat from escaping into space (like a literal lid).
items.random_wrong: They reflect sunlight back to Earth.
items.why: Students picture a physical blanket. The mechanism is selective absorption - greenhouse gases let visible light pass IN but absorb outgoing infrared. The re-emission warms the lower atmosphere and surface.

items.q: What does the 'tragedy of the commons' describe?
items.correct: Shared resources tend to be overexploited because individuals benefit personally while sharing costs.
items.misconception: It is a play about poor people.
items.why: Students take 'commons' as a place. Hardin's metaphor describes a STRUCTURAL PROBLEM: rational individual choices lead to collective ruin when costs are externalised - applies to overfishing, climate, and groundwater.
It's Intermittent. “Always on” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: No CO2 in use. “No money cost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many disadvantages were named (intermittent, land use, materials)?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
